summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his anthology 'The Best Software Writing I' by making a passionate case for better writing in the software industry. He illustrates the power of storytelling through a New York City anecdote and a military leadership story, contrasting vivid narrative with dry, abstract advice. He recounts refusing to endorse a book that was technically correct but fatally boring because it violated the 'show, don't tell' rule. The piece argues that software writing suffers from broken ESL, faux-academic pretentiousness, and a complete absence of narrative, and positions the anthology as a corrective showcase of genuinely good writing about software.
key_points: The 'show, don't tell' rule is the most important principle of good writing, and most software books violate it completely by offering abstract advice without stories or examples.
A vivid 400-word story about a Sergeant Major cleaning a toilet communicates leadership more effectively than any number of platitudes about 'setting a positive example.'
The software development world is drowning in poorly written material: bloated class library books by committee, pretentious academic texts, and juvenile online rants.
Technical correctness and clear prose are necessary but insufficient — without narrative and engagement, a book is unconvincing and unreadable.
Good writing about software exists but is scattered across blogs and articles, and deserves to be collected and showcased.
money_quotes: The author had violated the number one rule of good writing, the 'Show, don't tell' rule. There was not a single story in the book.
It was chock full of sentences like 'A good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.' What the eff?
I'll bet you'd rather sit through ten of those 400 word stories than have to listen to someone drone on about how 'a good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.'
If I have to read another 2000 page book about some class library written by 16 separate people in broken ESL, I'm going to flip out.

If I see another hardback book about object oriented models written with dense faux-academic pretentiousness, I'm not going to shelve it any more in the Fog Creek library: it's going right in the recycle bin.
The software development world desperately needs better writing.

New York is the kind of place where ten things happen to you every day on the way to the subway that would have qualified as interesting dinner conversation in Bloomington, Indiana, and you don't pay them any notice.
key_insight: Software writing fails not because authors lack technical knowledge but because they lack storytelling — showing through concrete narrative is infinitely more persuasive than telling through abstract platitudes.
